symptoms.symptom_name: Rapidly ascending unilateral lower-limb erythema (ankle to mid-calf in ~12 h)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Expanding, poorly demarcated erythema is the defining feature of cellulitis.
Stasis dermatitis is chronic and typically bilateral, not acutely ascending and unilateral.
Very rapid spread keeps necrotising infection on the differential until excluded in person.

symptoms.symptom_name: Fever, rigors and systemic malaise (systemic inflammatory response)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: cellulitis of the lower limb with systemic inflammatory response
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Systemic toxicity upgrades severity (Eron class II+) and mandates urgent assessment for possible sepsis.
High fever with rigors points to infection rather than uncomplicated venous thrombosis.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: acute contact dermatitis or insect-bite hypersensitivity reaction
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Local allergic reactions do not cause fever, rigors and systemic toxicity.

symptoms.symptom_name: Skin-break portal of entry (insect bite on the affected shin ~48 h earlier)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A breach in the skin barrier is the usual entry point for the causative bacteria.

symptoms.symptom_name: Hot, tightly swollen, exquisitely tender skin with inability to bear weight

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Warmth, tension, oedema and tenderness are the local hallmarks of soft-tissue infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: Negative necrotising-fasciitis screen (no pain out of proportion, no violaceous bullae, no crepitus, no cutaneous anaesthesia)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of the hard signs lowers the probability of necrotising infection but cannot exclude it remotely; several features require hands-on examination to assess reliably.
A negative necrotising screen with the infective picture supports uncomplicated (non-necrotising) cellulitis as the working diagnosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Peau d'orange (superficial dermal oedema) over the erythema

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Dermal oedema producing an orange-peel texture is characteristic of cellulitis/erysipelas; partially visible on video but confirmed on examination.
Regional lymphadenopathy reflects lymphatic spread of a limb infection; requires palpation to detect.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia (HR 112) with otherwise stable haemodynamics (BP 110/70, RR 20, SpO2 97%)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: cellulitis of the lower limb with systemic inflammatory response
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Tachycardia is a SIRS criterion; clinician-measured vitals are sealed — the AI Doctor infers systemic upset from reported fever/rigors and feeling unwell, not from a measured heart rate.

symptoms.symptom_name: Inflammatory markers, white cell count and blood cultures

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: cellulitis of the lower limb with systemic inflammatory response
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Raised inflammatory markers/WCC and any positive cultures support severity and guide IV-vs-oral antibiotic choice; hospital investigations never available in real time via telehealth.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person medical assessment with vital signs and limb examination (including border marking)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms extent, severity and haemodynamic stability, and allows examination-based screening for necrotising infection that cannot be done remotely.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Full blood count, CRP and blood cultures
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Quantify the systemic inflammatory response, support sepsis assessment and guide the oral-versus-IV antibiotic decision.
Consider to exclude concurrent DVT if clinical doubt remains after in-person assessment; not first-line and not to delay antibiotics.
